coredata.dc:title: N, P-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for carbendazim detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yixia
Huo, Danqun
Wu, Huixiang
Wang, Xianfeng
Yang, Junsheng
Bian, Minghong
Ma, Yi
Hou, Changjun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple, sensitive and reliable fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensing platform was established to determine carbendazim based on N, P-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). N, P-CQDs with strong green fluorescence were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal reaction and characterized by TEM, XRD, Raman spectrum, FT-IR and XPS. The green fluorescence of N, P-CQDs was easily quenched by Au NPs with the consequence of FRET. With the presence of carbendazim in the N, P-CQDs solutions, the quenched fluorescence intensity was reduced due to the specific interaction between carbendazim and Au NPs. Based on this strategy, N, P-CQDs and Au NPs were employed as a fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of carbendazim. Under the optimal conditions, a good linear range from 0.005 μM to 0.16 μM was obtained, with a low detection limit of 0.002 μM (3δ/S). This sensing platform showed excellent anti-interference capability for carbendazim in a complex system of other interferents. Furthermore, as-present sensing platform was successfully used for carbendazim detection in real samples, indicating the potential applications in carbendazim monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbendazim
Green fluorescence
N, P-doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Gold nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of cabendazim in real samples
Quantification of carbendazim by N, P-CQDs and Au NPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 31171684 ), Chongqing science and technology commission ( CSTC2015shmszxl20097 ), Graduate Scientific Research and Innovation Project of Chongqing ( CYB17037 ), the Science and Technology Support Program of Sichuan Province (No. 2013FZ0043 ), the workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01 and sharing fund of Chongqing university's large equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images (A) and the UV–vis absorption spectra (B) of Au NPs.
The HRTEM image (A); XRD pattern (B); and FT-IR spectrum (C) of N, P-CQDs.
XPS survey spectrum of N, P-CQDs (A), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (B), N 1 s (C), P 2 p (D).

UV–vis absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, P-CQDs (b, c) of N, P-CQDs. Inset shows the photographs of the obtained N, P-CQDs under visible light (1) and UV light (2).

(A) Fluorescence spectra: (a) N, P-CQDs; (b) N, P-CQDs and Au NPs; (c) N, P-CQDs and carbendazim; (d) N, P-CQDs, Au NPs and carbendazim. (B) Fluorescence decay plot of N, P-CQDs without and with Au NPs. (C) TEM images of Au NPs in the presence of carbendazim; (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum: (a) Au NPs; (b) Au NPs and N, P-CQDs; (c) Au NPs and carbendazim; (d) Au NPs, carbendazim and N, P-CQDs.
(A) Different concentration of Au NPs; (B) effect of different pH; (C) incubation time.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, P-CQDs with 0.22 nM Au NPs and the addition of different concentration of carbendazim (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.16, 0.42, 0.52, 1.05, 1.57 μM). (B) The corresponding plot of F1/F0 versus the concentration of carbendazim. Inset: the photograph shows linear region of the plot.

Fluorescence emission spectrum of N, P-CQDs with 0.22 nM Au NPs in the presence of different positively charges ions and pesticides. The concentration of carbendazim was set as 1.05 μM, while the concentration of each pesticide and positively charges ion was 5-fold and 10-fold of carbendazim, respectively.
The formation process of N, P-doped CQDs and the fluorescence detection of carbendazim via FRET.
Detection of carbendazim using different sensing platforms.

row.entry: Multi-walled carbon nanotubes-polymeric methyl red film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent detection of carbendazim in spiked samples.

head.ce:title: N, P-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for carbendazim detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biorheological Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Vascular Implants, Bioengineering College of Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biorheological Science and Technology of Ministry of Education
State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Vascular Implants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liquor Making Biology Technology and Application of Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Bioengineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liquor Making Biology Technology and Application of Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple, sensitive and reliable fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensing platform was established to determine carbendazim based on N, P-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). N, P-CQDs with strong green fluorescence were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal reaction and characterized by TEM, XRD, Raman spectrum, FT-IR and XPS. The green fluorescence of N, P-CQDs was easily quenched by Au NPs with the consequence of FRET. With the presence of carbendazim in the N, P-CQDs solutions, the quenched fluorescence intensity was reduced due to the specific interaction between carbendazim and Au NPs. Based on this strategy, N, P-CQDs and Au NPs were employed as a fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of carbendazim. Under the optimal conditions, a good linear range from 0.005 μM to 0.16 μM was obtained, with a low detection limit of 0.002 μM (3δ/S). This sensing platform showed excellent anti-interference capability for carbendazim in a complex system of other interferents. Furthermore, as-present sensing platform was successfully used for carbendazim detection in real samples, indicating the potential applications in carbendazim monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbendazim (methyl-1H-benzo[d]imidazol-2-ylcarbamate), a broad-spectrum benzimidazole fungicide, is extensively applied for the prevention and control of various diseases in crops, fruits and vegetables [1]. However, as a toxic agent, carbendazim may induce health hazard even at a low dose [2]. Some animal experiments implied that carbendazim is associated with male infertility [3,4]. Besides, carbendazim can keep its chemical stability in ambient environment for a long time because the benzimidazolic ring of carbendazaim is difficult to break [5,6]. Therefore, determination of trace carbendazim in the crops, fruits and vegetables is significantly important to human health and environmental protection.

To monitor the trace amount of carbendazim, many available techniques have been established, such as gas chromatography (GC) [7], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8–12], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LCMS) [13–18], and capillary electrophoresis [19]. Although above-mentioned techniques have provided the desirable results, most of them involve intrinsic disadvantages including complex preparation of sample, expensive apparatus and regents, time-consuming extraction procedure, limiting the application of these methods in real-time detection. Recently colorimetric sensors based on notable metal nanoparticles have emerged in environmental analysis due to the detection results can be recognized by naked eye [20–24]. Au NPs-based colorimetric sensors had been reported to measure some pesticides [25–28]. For example, Ma et al. [29] established a simple, sensitive sensor for carbendazim detection using Au NPs as a colorimetric reader. However, the detection limit of this method was high and the detection result observed by naked eye would result in some false color signal. To overcome these problems, we try to develop an ultrasensitive and reliable method for carbendazim detection.

Fluorescence methods attract more and more attention in recent years owing to its facile, rapid, ultrasensitive, selective determination. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a novel florescence material are frequently employed for most aspects of fluorescence analysis field [30–35], attributing to the superiorities of CQDs in easy preparation, good biocompatibility [36,37], tunable fluorescence excitation and emission [35], high aqueous solubility [38], excellent photostability [39], low cytotoxicity [40,41], and chemical inertness. Nevertheless, low quantum yield (QY) of CQDs is still a big drawback, which limits their applications. Using chemical doping CQDs with heteroatoms, such as N, S, P, B, F [42–46], is considered as an effective approach to modulate the band gap and electronic density of CQDs, which would enhance the QY and broaden the practical applications of CQDs. Furthermore, the effect detection of pesticides based on the doped CQDs have been studied [47–49]. For instance, Zou et al. [45] established a fluorescence sensor based on inner filter effect by employing blue fluorescence N, S-doped CQDs for fluazinam analysis and the detection limit of this method was 10 nM.

Herein, we combined N, P-CQDs and Au NPs as a fluorescent sensing platform to effective determination of carbendazim. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , N, P-CQDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal route using diethylenetriaminepenta (methylenephosphonic acid) (DAP) and m-phenylenediamine as the N and P sources. The as-prepared CQDs exhibited strong emission at 512 nm with the excitation of 440 nm. Au NPs as energy acceptor could obviously quenched the fluorescence of N, P-CQDs via FRET. However, when carbendazim was added into the N, P-CQDs aqueous solutions, the interaction between carbendazim and Au NPs led dispersed Au NPs to a state of aggregation, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CQDs is recovered. To the best of our knowledge, few relevant researches depending the utilization of the green fluorescence N, P-CQDs for carbendazim determination based on FRET were reported. The FRET-based green fluorescence N, P-CQDs presented here would be a potential sensing platform for carbendazim determination.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), Rhodamine B were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai,China). M-phenylenediamine, diethylenetriaminepenta (methylenephosphonicacid) (DAP), dialysis dag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000, fluazinam, glyphosate and malathion were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, NH4Cl, MnCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, ZnSO4, Cu (NO3)2 were supplied by Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Carbendazim, triadimefon, trichlorfon, fenitrothion, chlorpyrifos, aldicarb, deltamethrin were provided from Chongqing Entry Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau. All these chemical materials were analytical grade without further purification. Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solutions were prepared by mixing three acids, which include 2.71 mL H3PO4 (85%), 2.36 mLCH3COOH and 2.47 g H3BO3. Ultrapure water used in experiment was produced by a Millipore Direct-Q water system with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm (Molsheim, France).

Fluorescence spectrum measurements were performed on a LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America). The structure and morphology of N, P-CQDs and newly synthesized AuNPs were observed on a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (zeiss Libra 200, Carl Zeiss Jena, Germany). A Lamda-900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer) was used to achieve absorption spectra and measure absorbance. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra analysis was performed using a Nicolet 550 FT-IR spectrometer (Shi-madzu Scientific Instruments). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on a GG3144-JPS-9200 X-ray photoeletron spectrometer. Zeta potential was recorded on a Nano ZS90 zetasizer (Malvern Instruments,UK). The Raman spectrum was achieved on a Thermo Fischer DXR. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was measured using a Bruker AXS D5005. Emission lifetimes were determined on an Edinburgh analytical instrument (FLS 920 fluorescence spectrometer).

The N, P-doped carbon quantum dots were prepared according to previous report with small adjustment [50]. Briefly, 4.62 mmol m-phenylenediamine (0.5 g) and 4.95 mmol DAP (2.84 g) were added into 30 mL ultrapure water. Then the mixture was homogenized with ultrasonication and subsequently transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with the hydrothemal procedure at 200℃for 5 h. After cooling down the room temperature naturally, the dark-brown solutions were obtained. And the undissolving substance was removed by centrifugation with the speed of 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Then the supernatant was collected and treated with a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000) for 48 h to get rid of the unreacted small chemicals. Finally, the purified N, P-CQDs were obtained and stored at 4℃for the following experiment.

Au NPs were prepared by the method reported in the literature [51]. Specifically, 2 mL 25 mM chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) aqueous solutions were mixed with 198 mL of ultrapure water in a 500 mL flask. The mixture was heated to boil and simultaneously 6 mL of freshly prepared trisodium citrate solutions (10 mg/mL) were quickly added under vigorous stirring. When the color of the mixture changed to wine red, the aqueous solutions were continuously boiling for an additional 0.5 h. Finally, the obtained Au NPs were cooled to room temperature and stored at 4℃refrigerator for future use.

The carbendazim standard solutions were prepared by DMF and then diluted with water to obtain various concentration stock solutions. Briefly, 150 μL of Au NPs aqueous solutions and the different volumes of various concentration of carbendazim stock solutions were successively added into a 5-mL centrifugal tube and shaken thoroughly. After 5 min at room temperature, 2.75 mL N, P-CQDs prepared with B-R buffer solutions were added into the mixture. The fluorescence intensity at 512 nm was recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation of 440 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of cabendazim in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility and reliability of the fluorescence method in practical applications, cabbage and apple bought in local supermarket were chosen as the real samples for analysis of carbendazim. The sample solutions were prepared according to previous study with some slight modification [52]. Both the surface of cabbage and apple were sprayed with a certain concentration of carbendazim standard solutions. Spiked samples were cut into pieces and homogenised using a juice blender. About 5 g samples were added into 10 mL dichloromethane. After removing the insoluble impurities, the mixture solutions were treated with water bath for 5 min at 50 ℃. Finally, the obtained solutions were added into 2 mL DMF and then diluted with 10 mL ultrapure water. The detection process was moved by the fluorescent measurement for carbendazim.

Au NPs were synthesized via the reduction of chloroauric acid by sodium citrate. The as-prepared Au NPs was characterized by TEM and UV–vis spectroscopy. It can be seen clearly in Fig. 1 A, the obtained Au NPs are monodisperse, spherical in shape, and uniform in size with the grain diameter about 13 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Au NPs displays a maximum peak at 520 nm. The molar concentration of the as-prepared Au NPs was calculated to be 4.3 nM according to Lambert-Beer’s law.

The N, P-CQDs were easily obtained by using m-phenylenediamine and DAP as the reacted materials. The morphologies and microstructures of N, P-CQDs were characterized by TEM and XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the high-resolution TEM image indicates that the N, P-CQDs are well dispersive spots. The size of N, P-CQDs range from 3.74 to 7.83 nm with a mean particle diameter of 5.33 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 2B, XRD pattern shows the amorphous solid of as-prepared N, P-CQDs. The diffraction peak of N, P-CQDs is at 20.9°, which is attributed to the (001) facet of graphite, with a lattice distance of about 0.24 nm. As described in Fig. 2C, the functional groups on the surface of N, P-CQDs were presented by FT-IR spectra. An obvious absorption peak appears at 3390 cm−1, which is resulted from stretching vibrations of OH, NH, implying abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of N, P-CQDs. These functional groups can promote the hydrophilicity of the N, P-CQDs. The peak at 2330 cm−1 is in accord with the CNH+ group. The sharp peak at 1650 cm-1 corresponds to the characteristic vibration of CN, while the peak at 1450 cm-1 is from PO bond. The peaks at 1370 and 1290 cm-1 are assigned to the characteristic stretch of C–Nband and the stretching vibrations of C–O bond. Peaks appear at 1120, 1050 and 957 cm−1 are originated from the stretching vibration of PO, C–N or PN bond. Raman spectra is depicted in Fig. 2D. The disordered (D) band and crystalline (G) band of N, P-CQDs is found at 1340 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1, respectively. The two typical Raman scattering peaks are consistent with the presence of sp3 defects and sp2 hybridization of carbon [53]. XPS was performed to further confirm the chemical composition of the N, P-CQDs. According to Fig. 3 A, the XPS spectrum of N, P-CQDs displays the characteristic peaks at 285, 400 and 531 eV, which correspond to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. Additional peaks at 134 and 192 eV are probably assigned to P 2 p and P 2 s. The high resolution scan of the C 1 s region (Fig. 3B) shows four different peaks indicating the existence of three different chemical states in carbon. The peak at 284.90 eV is due to the graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of N, P-doped CQDs. The peak at 286.16 eV can be attributed to the sp3-bonded carbon with carbon (CC), nitrogen (C–N) and phosphorus (C–P). Peak at 288.02 eV is ascribed to C–O, while peak at 289.22 eV relates to OCO. The high resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3C) with peaks at 398.68, 399.82 and 401.80 eV is associated with the NH, C–NC and NC/N-N/N–P bands, respectively. High resolution of the P 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3D) of N, P-CQDs clearly exhibits two peaks, centered at 132.76 and 133.66 eV, which represent the PO and PC/P-N bonds. All of those analyses suggest N and P atom are successfully doped into the CQDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were carried out to explore the optical properties of N, P-CQDs. Fig. 4 a shows two typical UV–vis absorption peaks of N, P-CQDs at 294 and 457 nm, respectively. The absorption peak appears at 294 nm, which corresponds to the π→π* of CN or CC bond. The other absorption peak at 457 nm is linked with strong emission, attributing to the trapping of excited state energy by surface states. N, P-CQDs aqueous solutions present bright green fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm. Additionally, the optimal emission wavelength at 512 nm was obtained under the excitation of 440 nm (Fig. 4b, c). The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of N, P-CQDs was measured based on the comparative method [54], which uses Rhodamine B in absolute ethanol as a fluorescence standard. As shown in Table S1, the FLQY of N, P-CQDs was measured to be 32%, displaying strong fluorescent quantum efficiency.

As depicted in Scheme 1 and Figure 5A , the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs is effectively quenched with the addition of Au NPs. Previous work had been reported that Au NPs as a fluorescence quencher could effectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs via FRET [49]. To illuminate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by Au NPs, we firstly discussed the absorption band of Au NPs and the fluorescence spectra of N, P-CQDs. The absorption band of Au NPs at 520 nm overlaps with the fluorescence emission wavelength of N, P-CQDs after excitation of 440 nm. Furthermore, the electrostatic interaction between Au NPs and N, P-CQDs, shorten the distance of Au NPs and N, P-CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the zeta potential of N, P-CQDs is +17.7 mv, indicating the positive charge on the surface of N, P-CQDs, which is possibly attributed to the existence of -NH+- or -C = NH+- group on the surface of N, P-CQDs. On the contrary, the zeta potential of citrate-Au NPs is -34.2 mv, making clear that the surface charge of Au NPs is negative. The negatively charged Au NPs is due to the carboxyl group of citrate on the surface. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime is easily affected by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer process but has no obvious change by inner filter effect. Thus, the fluorescence lifetime of N, P-CQDs before and after the addition of Au NPs were measured. As can be seen in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence lifetime of N, P-CQDs in the absence and presence of Au NPs is 4.81 ns and 3.76 ns, respectively. This observation means that the fluorescence lifetime of N, P-CQDs was decayed after incubation with Au NPs, indicating the existence of FRET between N, P-CQDs and Au NPs.

As shown in Fig. 5A, there is no obvious difference for the green fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs with and without carbendazim, revealing that carbendazim not directly interacts with N, P-CQDs. Whereas, the quenched fluorescence of N, P-CQDs will reduce when the addition of carbendazim. According to previous work, carbendazim could interact with Au NPs through nitrogen atoms (-NH-/-N=) and the aromatic structure, resulting in the aggregation of Au NPs [29]. It could be demonstrated by the TEM images (Fig. 5C). UV–vis absorption spectra was carried out to further confirm this phenomenon. As seen from Fig. 5D, a new peak appears at 640 nm after adding the carbendazim, which suggests the interaction between Au NPs and carbendazim. Therefore, carbendazim can interact with Au NPs and induce the aggregation of Au NPs, and then redundant Au NPs quench the fluorescence of N, P-CQDs. Thus, an effective FRET sensing platform based on N, P-CQDs and Au NPs was developed for the detection of carbendazim.

Experimental parameters are of great importance in the detection process. In this sensing platform, the concentration of Au NPs, pH in medium and incubation time were considerable to optimize for fluorescence detection. F/F0 was described as the fluorescence quenching of N, P-CQDs aqueous solutions in the absence and presence of Au NPs. F was the blank of N, P-CQDs. F1/F0 was used for illustrating the fluorescence enhancement efficiency, where F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs in the presence of Au NPs and F1 represented the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs in the presence of Au NPs with the addition of carbendazim.

As an extraordinary fluorescence quencher, the effective quenching concentration of Au NPs was an important parameter for fluorescence assay. Fig. 6 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs was markedly reduced, accompanied with the increment of Au NPs concentration. When the concentration of Au NPs was 0.22 nM, the quenching efficiency of N, P-CQDs was to be 89.40%. Therefore, 0.22 nM of Au NPs was applied for the following experiment.

As displayed in Fig. 6B, pH of B–R buffer solutions was investigated. It is obvious that F1/F0 value gradually increased with the increase of pH from 4 to 6. The F1/F0 value was reached maximum as pH was at 6. However, in the pH range from 6.5 to 9, the F1/F0 value decreased gradually. The functional group in the surface of Au NPs was rich in carboxyl, which resulted from synthetic process by using trisodium citrate as both reducing and protecting agent. The carboxyl group in the surface of Au NPs probably tended to protonation when the pH values were less than 6. It would result in the fluorescence quenching effect weakening. However, under the alkaline medium, the carboxyl group of Au NPs was likely to affect by OH−, which declines the fluorescence quenching effect. Hence, pH 6 was the best choice for the carbendazim determination.

We further discussed the suitable incubation time. As shown in Fig. 6C, the F1/F0 value gradually increased as the incubation time increasing. And the reaction system was reached a relative equilibrium state after 5 min of incubation time. As a result, 5 min was chosen as the incubation time of carbendazim and Au NPs and there was no needed any further incubation when the N, P-CQDs was adding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of carbendazim by N, P-CQDs and Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the sensitivity of the fluorescent sensing platform, a series of carbendazim concentration were added under the optimal experimental conditions. As Fig. 7 showed, introducing of carbendazim would reduce the quenching of the fluorescence of N, P-CQDs. Accompanied with the concentration of carbendazim ranging from 0 to 1.57 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs at 512 nm gradually achieved to maximum. A linear calibration curve between F1/F0 and the concentration of carbendazim in the range of 0.005 μM to 0.16 μM exhibited a commendable linear relationship with the correlation coefficients is 0.9945. The equation of calibration plot was expressed as F1/F0 = 0.14 c+1.46 (c: μM). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.002 μM, which was calculated by using three times the standard deviation rule (3δ/S). Compared with other sensing platforms for the detection of carbendazim in the literature was shown in Table 1 . Apparently, the limit of detection obtained in this sensing platform displayed a high sensitivity to carbendazim.

In order to research the specificity of the proposed sensing platform towards carbendazim, the reaction system was performed in some disrupting substances including positively charges ions such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, and some common amino acid such as cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, tryptophan, histidine, alanine, isoleucine, valine, serine, threonine, agedoite, arginine, as well as other pesticides, for instance, fluazinam, triadimefon, glyphosate, trichlorfon, fenitrothion, chlopyrifos, aldicarb and deltamethrin, under the same experimental conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 8 and Fig. S3, among of these high concentration interfering substances had no obvious influence on the reaction system. Only carbendazim could lead to significantly increase the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs owing to the specific interaction between carbendazim and Au NPs. The result substantiated that the as-developed sensing platform based on N, P-CQDs and Au NPs is potentially applied for carbendazim determination.

The fluorescent sensing platform based on N, P-CQDs and Au NPs was applied for carbendazim determination in fruits (apple) and vegetables (cabbage) to evaluate the feasibility and applicability in real world. After spiking a series of certain concentration of carbendazim in the samples, the analysis procedure was conducted by the aforesaid method. When each concentration was measured by three parallel trials, the average recovery value was calculated by the calibration curve. As illustrated in the Table 2 , the recoveries of apple and cabbage samples ranged from 83.79% to 115.31% and 86.51% to 110.47%, respectively. And the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the analytical samples were lower than 8.74%. On the basis of the obtained results, the established sensing platform exhibits the satisfied behavior in carbendazim detection for real samples.

In conclusion, we proposed a highly sensitive and reliable fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of carbendazim based on FRET between N, P-CQDs and Au NPs. N, P-CQDs were prepared through a simple and facile one-step hydrothermal treatment and exhibited strong green fluorescence. Au NPs could easily quench the fluorescence of N, P-CQDs as a result of FRET. After introducing of carbendazim, the fluorescence quenching of N, P-CQDs was reduced due to the interaction between carbendazim and Au NPs. Based on the mechanism, the proposed sensing platform had a fine liner relationship with 0.005 to 0.16 μM and the limit of detection was down to 0.002 μM. Most importantly, the proposed sensing platform not only exbibited remarkable selectivity, but also applied to real samples detection with satisfactory accuracy. All these results displayed a promising application of this sensing platform in carbendazim monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 31171684), Chongqing science and technology commission (CSTC2015shmszxl20097), Graduate Scientific Research and Innovation Project of Chongqing (CYB17037), the Science and Technology Support Program of Sichuan Province (No. 2013FZ0043), the workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01 and sharing fund of Chongqing university's large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.130.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HPLC method for simultaneous determination of fungicides: carbendazim, metalaxyl, folpet, and propiconazole in must and wine
J. Agric. Food Chem.
Toxic effects of carbendazim at low dose levels in male rats
J. Toxicol. Sci.
Embryolethal and teratogenic effects of carbendazim in rats
Teratog., Carcinog. Mutagen.
Ex vivo assessment of testicular toxicity induced by carbendazim and iprodione, alone or in a mixture
Altex
Dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction combined with high performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detection for the determination of carbendazim and thiabendazole in environmental samples

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yixia Yang is a graduate in Bioengineering College of Chongqing University, China. She focus on preparation of quantum dots and its application in pesticides trace sensing.

Danqun Huo received pH.D. degree in biological medical engineering from Chongqing University in 2004 and had once been visited Australia, America and Italy. She is currently Distinguished Professor of Sichuan University of science and engineering and professor of biomedical science of Chongqing University. Her current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and sensor-array, electrochemistry and analytical microsystems.

Changjun Hou completed his pH.D. in biomedical engineering in 2004 at Chongqing University. After a period of research at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign as a visiting scholar, he returned to China and got tenure at the college of bioengineering in Chongqing University. In 2010, he became a full professor in biomedical engineering. His scientific interests rest on the design and construction of biochemical sensing systems based on porphyrins and other sensitive materials.
